schools.summary: The command to pour out the vials: 'and I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth' — a great voice from the temple (from God, 15:8) commanding the seven angels to pour out their bowls of wrath (the order given for the final judgments to begin); the angels bidden to 'pour out the vials (phialas — bowls) of the wrath of God upon the earth' (the seven last plagues, ch. 15, now to be emptied on the earth, the command initiating the series of vial-judgments); the voice coming 'out of the temple' (the command for the judgments proceeding from God's sanctuary, 15:5-8, the vials of wrath poured out by divine authority, the judgments God's own appointed acts) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth
the command to pour out the vials of wrath
the seven last plagues ordered from God's sanctuary
the final judgments by divine authority

schools.summary: The first vial: 'and the first went, and poured out his vial upon the earth; and there fell a noisome and grievous sore upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and upon them which worshipped his image' — the first angel pouring his bowl on the earth, a foul, painful sore breaking out (the plague recalling the boils of Egypt, Exodus 9:9-11, the vials paralleling the Egyptian plagues as judgments on a new Egypt/Babylon); the 'noisome (evil, foul) and grievous (painful) sore' a painful, loathsome ulcer or affliction (whether literal or, in the historicist/symbolic reading, a moral and spiritual plague, a corrupting, tormenting judgment on the followers of the beast); the sore falling specifically 'upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and... worshipped his image' (the plague directed at the beast-worshippers, ch. 13, the judgment falling on the adherents of the antichristian power) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: the first went, and poured out his vial upon the earth; and there fell a noisome and grievous sore
upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and upon them which worshipped his image
the first vial bringing a grievous sore on the beast-worshippers
the judgment targeted at the antichristian power's followers

schools.summary: The second vial: 'and the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea' — the second angel pouring his bowl on the sea, which becomes like the blood of a corpse (dark, clotted, putrid), and every living thing in the sea dying (the plague recalling the first Egyptian plague, Exodus 7:20-21, the Nile turned to blood, and the second trumpet, 8:8-9); 'as the blood of a dead man' not fresh blood but the dark, corrupt blood of a corpse (the figure of utter corruption and death, whether literal or symbolic of a judgment corrupting and destroying in the realm signified by the sea, the nations); 'every living soul (every living thing) died in the sea' all sea-life perishing (the plague bringing complete death to the sea, the figure of a sweeping, deadly judgment, the second vial extending the wrath from the land to the sea) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man
every living soul died in the sea
the second vial turning the sea to blood
the sweeping, deadly judgment extending the wrath to the sea

schools.summary: The third vial: 'and the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood' — the third angel pouring his bowl on the rivers and springs, which become blood (the plague extending the bloody judgment from the sea, v.3, to the fresh waters, again recalling the Egyptian plague, Exodus 7:19-20, and the third trumpet, 8:10-11); the plague falling on 'the rivers and fountains of waters' (the sources of fresh water and drink, the judgment now striking the very springs and rivers, whether literal or symbolic of a corrupting judgment on the sources of life/sustenance in the realm of the beast); 'they became blood' (the rivers and springs turning to blood, fitting the retribution declared in vv.5-6, blood for those who shed the blood of the saints, the waters of drink becoming blood, a judgment answering the bloodshed of the persecutors) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood
the third vial turning the rivers to blood
the bloody judgment extended to the fresh waters
a fitting retribution for the blood of the saints

schools.summary: The angel of the waters' vindication: 'and I heard the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus' — the angel set over the waters (the agent or guardian of that element) declaring God righteous in this judgment (the eternal God, who is, was, and shall be, justified in turning the waters to blood); the angel addressing the eternal God (the title from 1:4, 8, here 'which art, and wast, and shalt be,' the osios — holy one), affirming God's righteousness (even the dreadful bloody judgment owned as just by the heavenly ministers); God righteous 'because thou hast judged thus' (the very judgment, the waters to blood, the ground of the praise, the retribution, explained in v.6, just, the angel vindicating God's justice in the plague far from questioning it) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be
because thou hast judged thus
the angel of the waters vindicating God's righteous judgment
the very judgment owned as just by heaven

schools.summary: The retribution of blood: 'for they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy' — because the persecutors shed the blood of the saints and prophets, God giving them blood to drink (the waters turned to blood, the punishment fitting the crime, those who shed blood given blood, the lex talionis of divine justice); 'they have shed the blood of saints and prophets' the crime (they killed God's faithful people and messengers, the bloodshed of the martyrs the ground of the bloody retribution, the cry of the martyrs, 6:10, answered); 'thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy (axioi — deserving)' the fitting penalty (the retribution exactly fitting, blood for bloodshed, and fully deserved, the persecutors 'worthy' of this judgment, God's justice repaying the shedders of blood with blood) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy
the retribution of blood for the persecutors of the saints
the fitting, deserved retribution, blood for blood
the martyrs' cry answered

schools.summary: The altar's confirmation: 'and I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments' — a voice from the altar (perhaps the altar under which the martyrs' souls cried, 6:9-10) responding and confirming the angel's vindication (vv.5-6), 'Even so' (yea, amen) affirming that God's judgments are true and righteous; 'another out of the altar' perhaps the voice of the martyrs (6:9-10) who had cried 'how long?', now confirming that their blood is avenged (the altar of the martyrs answering, vindicating God's judgment); 'true and righteous are thy judgments' the doxology of justice (God's judgments true — faithful, according to truth — and righteous — just, even the severest plagues owned as true and righteous, heaven repeatedly vindicating God's justice in the vials, cf. 15:3) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments
the altar's confirmation of God's true judgments
the martyrs' altar confirming God's just judgment
heaven vindicating God's justice in the vials

schools.summary: The fourth vial: 'and the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire' — the fourth angel pouring his bowl on the sun, which is given power to scorch men with fire (the sun, instead of giving benign light, becoming an instrument of burning judgment, the plague of intense, scorching heat); the vial on 'the sun' turning the source of light and life into an agent of torment (whether literal scorching heat or, symbolically, a judgment turning what should bless into what afflicts, the fourth plague striking through the sun); 'power was given unto him to scorch men with fire' (the sun permitted to burn men with fierce heat, 'was given' marking the divine permission, the scorching a tormenting judgment on the impenitent, yet, v.9, leading not to repentance but to blasphemy) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire
the fourth vial scorching men with the sun's heat
the source of light turned to an instrument of burning judgment
the tormenting plague on the impenitent

schools.summary: The impenitent response: 'and men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues: and they repented not to give him glory' — scorched by the great heat, men blaspheming God (who controls the plagues) instead of repenting (the judgment hardening rather than softening them, they did not give God glory); 'blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues' (they reviled God, acknowledging even in their blasphemy that he controls the plagues, yet hating rather than submitting to him, the impenitent railing against the God who judges them); 'they repented not to give him glory' (the plague not bringing repentance, they refusing to turn and glorify God, the hardening of the impenitent under judgment as 9:20-21, judgment alone without grace not softening the rebellious heart but provoking blasphemy) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues
they repented not to give him glory
men blaspheming rather than repenting under the scorching
judgment hardening rather than softening the rebellious

schools.summary: The fifth vial: 'and the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness; and they gnawed their tongues for pain' — the fifth angel pouring his bowl directly on the throne (thronos) of the beast, the beast's kingdom plunged into darkness (the plague striking at the very center of the antichristian power, recalling the Egyptian darkness, Exodus 10:21-23); the vial on 'the seat (throne) of the beast' striking the seat of his power, 'his kingdom full of darkness' (the darkness, literal or symbolic of confusion, calamity, and the eclipse of the beast's power, overspreading his realm, in the historicist reading the darkening and weakening of the antichristian/papal power); 'they gnawed their tongues for pain (anguish)' (the subjects of the beast's kingdom gnawing their tongues in agony, the figure of intense pain and distress under the plague, the darkness bringing torment as well as confusion) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness
they gnawed their tongues for pain
the fifth vial darkening the beast's kingdom
the judgment striking the center of the antichristian power

schools.summary: The renewed impenitence: 'and blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds' — under the fifth plague, men again blaspheming the God of heaven because of their pains (the darkness, v.10) and their sores (the first plague, v.2, still afflicting them), and again not repenting (the hardening deepening); 'blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores' (they reviling God for their sufferings, the accumulating plagues provoking not repentance but blasphemy, they cursing God for the very judgments their sins have brought); 'repented not of their deeds' (they would not repent of their evil deeds, the refrain, vv.9, 11, stressing the obstinate hardness of the impenitent, the plagues however severe not bringing the rebellious to repentance, the heart unchanged by grace only hardening under wrath) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds
the renewed blasphemy and impenitence under the fifth plague
the accumulating judgments provoking blasphemy, not repentance
the obstinate hardness of the impenitent

schools.summary: The sixth vial: 'and the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up, that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared' — the sixth angel pouring his bowl on the Euphrates, which dries up (the drying of the great river removing a barrier, as the drying of the Red Sea and Jordan, in the historicist reading the decline and removal of the Turkish/Ottoman power, the obstacle to the gospel in the East); 'that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared' (the drying opening a way for 'the kings of the east,' kings from the rising of the sun); interpreters varying — the historicists reading it of the way prepared for the conversion of the eastern nations (or the Jews) to the gospel, or for the powers that overthrow antichristian Babylon; the futurist reading literal kings from the east advancing for the final battle (vv.14, 16); the providential opening for God's purposes, whether judgment on Babylon or the ingathering of the nations (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up
that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared
the sixth vial drying the Euphrates for the kings of the east
the barrier removed for the appointed advance (the Ottoman fall, or kings for the final battle)

schools.summary: The three unclean spirits: 'and I saw three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and out of the mouth of the beast, and out of the mouth of the false prophet' — three unclean spirits, like frogs, issuing from the mouths of the dragon (Satan), the beast (the persecuting power), and the false prophet (the second beast) — the unholy trinity sending forth its lying, seducing spirits; 'like frogs' recalling the Egyptian plague of frogs (Exodus 8) and marking them as loathsome, unclean; the spirits coming from the 'mouth' of the three (from their lying speech, propaganda, and deceiving influence, the dragon, beast, and false prophet together sending forth the spirits that deceive the nations, v.14, the three forms of the antichristian power united in deception); the spirits 'like frogs' unclean, croaking, loathsome (the figure of foul, deceiving influences, whether demonic spirits or the lying doctrines and propaganda that go forth from the antichristian powers to gather the world to battle) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and out of the mouth of the beast, and out of the mouth of the false prophet
the three frog-like unclean spirits from the unholy trinity
the lying, seducing spirits of the antichristian powers
loathsome deceivers gathering the world to battle

schools.summary: The demonic gathering: 'for they are the spirits of devils, working miracles, which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle of that great day of God Almighty' — the frog-like spirits (v.13) demonic spirits that work signs to deceive (the lying wonders, as 13:13-14; 2 Thessalonians 2:9, by which they seduce the kings of the earth); they 'go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle' (deceiving and assembling the kings of the whole world for the great final conflict against God and his people, the demonic deception uniting the world's powers for the last battle); 'the battle of that great day of God Almighty' the climactic conflict on the great day of God's judgment (the assembling of the antichristian powers for the final battle, Armageddon, v.16, which ends in their overthrow, the great day when God Almighty triumphs over his gathered enemies) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: the spirits of devils gather the kings of the world to the battle of the great day of God Almighty — the final assembling of the enemies of God for the last conflict, which the Almighty turns to their destruction; the great day of God's decisive judgment, when the antichristian powers, deceived and gathered, are overthrown.

themes: they are the spirits of devils, working miracles, which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world
to gather them to the battle of that great day of God Almighty
the demonic spirits gathering the kings to the final battle
the world's powers assembled for the conflict that ends in God's triumph

schools.summary: Christ's interjected call: 'Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked, and they see his shame' — amid the gathering for battle, Christ suddenly speaking, warning that he comes unexpectedly, as a thief in the night (Matthew 24:43; 1 Thessalonians 5:2; Revelation 3:3), the suddenness of his coming calling for constant readiness; 'Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments' the blessing (blessed the one who stays awake, spiritually vigilant, and keeps his garments — his readiness, purity, righteousness, like one dressed and prepared, the watchful, ready believer blessed at Christ's coming); 'lest he walk naked, and they see his shame' the warning (the unwatchful in danger of being caught unprepared, found naked, stripped of righteousness/readiness, and exposed to shame, the call to be ready, clothed in righteousness, lest Christ's sudden coming find one shamefully unprepared) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments
lest he walk naked, and they see his shame
Christ's interjected call to watchfulness
the sudden coming calling for constant readiness

schools.summary: The gathering at Armageddon and the name's meaning: 'and he gathered them together into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon' — the demonic spirits (vv.13-14) gathering the kings of the earth into one place for the great final battle (the gathering the climax of the demonic deception); 'Armageddon' (Greek Harmagedon) from the Hebrew 'Har Megiddo' — the 'Mountain (or hill) of Megiddo,' Megiddo in the plain of Esdraelon/Jezreel a famous battlefield (Judges 5:19, Barak and Sisera; 2 Chronicles 35:22; 2 Kings 23:29-30, where Josiah was slain by Pharaoh-necho; Zechariah 12:11, the mourning of Hadadrimmon in the valley of Megiddon), the name marking a place of decisive battle and great slaughter; 'Armageddon' taken as the symbolic name for the place of the final, decisive conflict between the powers of evil and God (whether a literal location or, more commonly, a symbol drawn from the historic battlefield of Megiddo, signifying the great climactic battle where the antichristian powers are gathered and overthrown, the name evoking decisive divine victory) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

debates.question: What is Armageddon, and what does the name signify?

sides.position: Har-Megiddo, the 'Mountain of Megiddo' — the historic battlefield (Judges 5; 2 Kings 23; Zechariah 12), taken symbolically as the place of the great final, decisive conflict
A literal future location where the kings of the earth are gathered for the last battle

themes: he gathered them together into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon
the gathering of the kings at Armageddon
Har-Megiddo, the Mountain of Megiddo, the historic battlefield
the symbolic place of the great final conflict

schools.summary: The seventh vial and the declaration: 'and the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air; and there came a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done' — the seventh and last angel pouring his bowl into the air (the realm of the powers of the air, Ephesians 2:2, or the all-encompassing element, the final plague completing the wrath, 15:1); 'a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done (gegonen — it is finished/come to pass)' — a great voice from the throne in the heavenly temple declaring the outpouring of God's wrath completed (the consummation of the judgments announced, the end come); 'It is done' the word of completion (the wrath of God filled up, 15:1, the judgments finished, the declaration from the throne marking the consummation of God's purpose against the antichristian world, what God appointed now accomplished) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air; and there came a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done
the seventh vial and the great voice declaring It is done
the final plague completing the wrath
the consummation of God's judgments announced from the throne

schools.summary: The unprecedented earthquake: 'and there were voices, and thunders, and lightnings; and there was a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty an earthquake, and so great' — the seventh vial accompanied by voices, thunders, lightnings, and a great earthquake (the greatest ever known, the convulsions of nature marking the consummating judgment); 'voices, and thunders, and lightnings' (as 4:5; 8:5; 11:19) the tokens of God's majestic, awful presence in judgment (the storm of divine wrath attending the final plague); 'a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty... and so great' an unprecedented, supreme convulsion (the figure of a vast, final overthrow and upheaval, shaking the whole order of the antichristian world, the greatest of all judgments, beyond any before it) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: there were voices, and thunders, and lightnings; and there was a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth
the unprecedented earthquake at the seventh vial
the tokens of God's awful presence in the consummating judgment
the supreme upheaval shaking the antichristian world

schools.summary: The fall of Babylon: 'and the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell: and great Babylon came in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath' — the great city (Babylon) split into three parts (broken, disintegrated), and the cities of the nations falling (the earthquake, v.18, toppling the antichristian city and the cities of the world, the collapse of the structures of the antichristian order); 'great Babylon came in remembrance before God' (God remembering Babylon, her sins, 18:5, bringing her to the long-deserved judgment, the time of her reckoning come, God's remembrance of her crimes issuing in her punishment); 'the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath' the cup of his fierce anger (Jeremiah 25:15; Revelation 14:10, she who made the nations drink her wine of fornication, 14:8, now drinking the cup of God's wrath, the fitting retribution on the great corrupt city) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell
great Babylon came in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath
the fall of Babylon and the cities under the seventh vial
the corrupt city brought to her long-deserved reckoning

schools.summary: The cosmic upheaval: 'and every island fled away, and the mountains were not found' — under the great earthquake (v.18), every island fleeing and the mountains disappearing (the figure of a total, sweeping upheaval, the most stable and permanent features of the earth removed, the convulsion overturning the whole established order); 'every island fled away' the islands vanishing as if fleeing (the figure, drawn from 6:14, of the dissolution of the existing order, whether literal or symbolic of the overthrow of nations and kingdoms); 'the mountains were not found' the mountains, the symbols of stability and permanence, disappearing (the figure of the complete overthrow of the established powers and structures, nothing of the old order standing, the consummating judgment leveling all) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: every island fled away, and the mountains were not found
the islands and mountains vanishing
the total convulsion overthrowing the established order
nothing of the old order standing

schools.summary: The great hail and final blasphemy: 'and there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent: and men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for it was exceeding great' — a great hail falling, each stone about a talent in weight (very heavy, 50-100 pounds), the plague of enormous, crushing hailstones (recalling the Egyptian hail, Exodus 9:23-24, and the seventh trumpet, 11:19, the final, devastating judgment); each hailstone 'about the weight of a talent' enormous and destructive (the figure of a terrible, crushing judgment, whether literal hail or symbolic of a devastating outpouring of wrath); 'men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for it was exceeding great' (even under this last, dreadful plague, men blaspheming God rather than repenting, the impenitent to the very end hardening themselves and cursing God, the refrain of blasphemy, vv.9, 11, closing the vials, judgment alone to the last not softening the rebellious) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent
men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for it was exceeding great
the great hail and the final blasphemy of the impenitent
the impenitent hardened to the very end
